speaker: interviewer
text: Erna, would you please tell us the significance of this book that we're looking at now, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I went back to Germany in 1981 for the exhibit for the museum-- opening of the museum, some people apparently put together this booklet about the six of us who-- six women who escaped from a camp. The picture on the booklet is supposed to be one of the camps in Essen. I don't-- it was not really the camp I was in but was one of the camps in Essen. But in the booklet, it describes the camp we were in, what we did, and how we escaped. So I was given this book when I was in Germany for the opening of the Jewish Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Erna, the mishpocheh is in place, and everybody would like to talk to you. Would you be kind enough to introduce your lovely family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here's my husband, Bernie, my daughter, Miriam, my son, Jonathan, Jonathan's wife, Susan, my youngest grandson, Daniel, and my only granddaughter, Jennifer, my middle grandson, Joshua, and next to Joshua is my son-in-law, Elliott Cooperman.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one-- one thing I wanted to mention.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one grandson is missing, David Cooperman.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- when I was talking to my wife, Erna, I found that there are certain things she left out. Like for instance, she wanted to mention that when she was once kicked in the behind during a-- did you mention that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was for my 21st birthday. There was an air-- there was a bombing-- air raid, and I thought, I'll sneak out and get a drink of water. And the SS men caught me and kicked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I remember my 21st birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she-- she-- she told me about it. And that's, of course, quite a bit later. And Erna said that you don't-- you won't feel it, because before I leave this camp, I'm gonna shoot you dead. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I already told them that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You told them that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. All right. That's all I wanted to mention. You want to take it back?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do feel-- how do you feel about your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's the most intelligent person I've ever met in my life. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know that 'cause she married you, right? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a wonderful marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something to your mom? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I don't know if it's going to work. [INTERPOSING VOICES] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's the box of tissues?

speaker: interviewer
text: The tissues--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that this interview that you just did-- the-- the one trait that I think is the most important about my mother has come through, and it's the trait that I think got her through the Holocaust, that allowed her to establish such a good life in America and [SOBBING] raise two successful children. And I'm not going-- [SOBBING] [LAUGHTER] and make her as loved and admired by everyone she meets as she is, and that is her independence-- her strong sense of-- of independence. And I know it started much earlier, when she tells me stories about her tomboy childhood. And although I know she feels that she survived the Holocaust just from pure luck, I know that [SIGHS] the, you know, the contacts that she made, the-- [SOBBING] the ability, the daring, the escaping from concentration camp is just another manifestation of this. And I think it's the same spirit of independence that allowed her to get reestablished in Germany without the post-war agencies that made her leave the auspices of her aunt in Pittsburgh and strike out on her own in New York. And that same sense that allowed my brother and I to be such successful adults. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Most wonderful. [SOBS] Just wonderful, wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I hope-- I feel that I've led a very charmed life compared to hers. And I hope that if I ever am tested the way you were, I would be able to meet those challenges.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope you never get tested, sweetheart. I hope you're never, never tested. I hope you're never tested. But I-- I was-- I have the most wonderful family. And I keep telling everybody, I have two children who have never in their lives-- there wasn't a day when they did something that I was upset about. [LAUGHTER] I mean, they are just wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I wanted to read it. I thought I'd be better if I read it, but it would have been just as bad. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. And I-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] and when I was in camp, I didn't care if I survive or not. But now that I did survive, I think I'm so lucky that I did, because I have the family that I have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, good. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. All right, your turn. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure I'm quite as eloquent as-- as Miriam was, but what she said was very, very accurate. There's a life force about my mother, which is just-- I think radiates from her and makes her very much liked by other people. She makes friends very easily. She remembers-- has a amazing memory for people, remembers people years and years later, which I admire. But I think that life force has come through, I think, both to my sister and I. And I certainly hope that we can pass it on to, you know, my daughter and, and-- I'm crying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't cry. He's [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. But I just-- certainly the most, most wonderful mother I could have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: --possibly imagined.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have two wonderful, two wonderful kids, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Susan your turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I just wanted to say that [CRYING] being that my mother died when I was six years old, this is really the only mother that I, that I ever knew. And she's the most incredible person I've ever met.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you, Susan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I could think of one word to describe her, is just-- she's just very strong physically, emotionally. And I don't know whether it's because of the experiences during the war or in spite of the experiences, but she doesn't complain about anything. She never complains she's too tired to do anything. She never complains she's too tired to play with my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: She just has the-- an amazing amount of energy I've, I've-- and she's my role model. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you, Susan. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I come over here to sleep over, I really enjoy it. We watch together every night. Well, whenever we get the newspaper, I help her with the puzzles. She usually does all the thinking, though. [LAUGHTER] She takes me to her pool during the summer. She gave me the courage to go off the high dive. And I just really love her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, sweetheart. [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHTER] [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandma is one of the most important people in my lives. And whenever I'm here, she always listens to me when I talk about physics or biking. And even though I'm the only one talking, she'll still listen and make an input. And she also makes the best stuffed, stuffed cabbage [LAUGHTER] there is. And that's why I love her. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Jennifer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my grandma, and I think she was very lucky to get away from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold up the picture, Jen. Into the camera, Jen. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say something about, again, about using John's words about life force and Miriam's words about independence and add the word resilience. When I first met my wife, Miriam, in-- [LAUGHTER] [SIGHS] --her apartment was a, a poster and it said, if life gives you lemons, make lemonade. And the more I thought about that, I really feel like there's, there's a lot in-- that, that said to me a lot about my wife's qualities and, and-- which I think are so much from her mother and her mother's experiences. And I, I think Erna has that special resilience which she, she lives her life with and has been a model to all of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to read something. My, my oldest son, David, is sick today, and so he couldn't come. But he wrote a few words. My grandma has always played an active role in my life. She helped me enjoy bicycling and walking and learning. She continually wants the most out of life for herself and those around her. [SOBBING] This guiding principle has made me a better, more aware person. I love her dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you it's  a bicycling family. [LAUGHTER]
